SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments (Kodak and classic images) and an implemented entropy coder, and the continuous relaxation (additive uniform noise) is a standard, mathematically plausible approach enabling SGD. Mathematical approach and empirical validation appear correct, but there are minor technical issues: the differential-entropy bias for coarse quantization is acknowledged but not fully characterized, architectural choices are somewhat ad hoc, and performance does not uniformly beat JPEG 2000. Overall the evidence supports the paper’s conclusions with only minor gaps that do not undermine the main contributions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is very well organized with a clear flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (end-to-end nonlinear transform coding, GDN nonlinearity, and the continuous relaxation for quantization) are stated early. The method section cleanly explains the transforms, additive uniform-noise relaxation, and the rate–distortion objective, while experiments report actual bit rates (entropy coder implementation), datasets (ImageNet subset, Kodak, classic images), and multiple metrics (MSE/PSNR, MS-SSIM), making results easy to follow. Notation and definitions are consistently presented, figures/tables and examples (as implied by the reported evaluations) are informative, and the writing is excellent with explicit discussion of limitations, which further improves clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a clearly new end-to-end learned image compression pipeline by jointly optimizing convolutional analysis/synthesis transforms with a GDN nonlinearity and by using an additive uniform-noise relaxation to enable SGD through quantization—techniques not standard in classic codecs. It also implements an actual entropy coder and empirically validates that the continuous relaxation approximates discrete quantization, differentiating it from prior analytical or hand-engineered transform codecs (JPEG, JPEG 2000). These elements constitute a non-trivial methodological advance and would be informative to experts, though they build on existing ideas in transform coding and probabilistic modeling rather than overturning the field entirely.

main_claims: Introduce a complete end-to-end optimized image compression method based on cascades of convolutional layers and a generalized divisive normalization (GDN) nonlinearity.
Train analysis and synthesis transforms jointly for rate–distortion performance using a continuous relaxation (additive uniform noise) to enable stochastic gradient descent despite quantization.

Demonstrate improved perceptual quality (MS-SSIM) and generally better rate–distortion performance (PSNR/MSE) than JPEG and JPEG 2000 for most test images and bit rates, with evaluation using actual bit rates via an implemented entropy coder.

Provide an entropy coding implementation (CABAC-based) and empirical validation that the continuous relaxation approximates discrete quantization reasonably well (bias decreases for finer quantization).
_summary.methodology: Build parametric analysis and synthesis transforms from cascades of convolutions, down/upsampling and GDN/IGDN nonlinearities, apply uniform scalar quantization in code space, and jointly optimize transform and marginal probability models by replacing quantization with additive uniform noise and minimizing a rate–distortion loss L = −E log P + λ E d via stochastic gradient descent.
datasets: Classic test images: Lena, Barbara, Peppers, Mandrill
metrics: Bit rate / actual bits (via entropy coding)
limitations: The continuous (differential) entropy estimate is positively biased for coarse quantization, though bias disappears for finer quantization (stated empirically).
Method does not outperform JPEG 2000 on every image and every bit rate (performance varies across images and rates).
Training is computationally expensive (high computational costs for training models).
Architectural choices (filter counts, sizes, etc.) are somewhat ad-hoc and not fully explored; authors consider some conclusions provisional.
Perceptual transform for color images was not used because a reliable perceptual metric for color images was not available; models were trained with MSE instead.
_summary.topic_area: Image compression - nonlinear transform coding / end-to-end optimization
